title: Cinnamon spice cold-process soap
subtitle: A warm-toned bar with cinnamon bark and clove bud essential oils at low skin-safe concentrations
excerpt: Cinnamon bark and clove bud are skin sensitisers and must be used well below their standard maximum in a rinse-off soap. This bar uses both at a combined 0.5% of oil weight, which gives a subtle warm spice note without the sensitisation risk at higher rates. Natural cinnamon powder adds flecks of colour throughout the bar.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat; 33% lye discount. Cinnamon bark EO IFRA rinse-off limit: 0.05% as a leave-on, higher for rinse-off but sensitisation risk remains high above 0.5% combined with clove bud. Reference: IFRA standard 49th amendment. Clove bud EO in soap: known to accelerate trace significantly; add at the last moment before pouring.

recipe.batchNotes: Recalculate lye for any change to oil weight using a lye calculator.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: 48 hours in the mould; 4 to 6 weeks on a curing rack. Not suitable as a facial soap; keep to body use given the spice essential oils.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years in a cool dark place. Store on an open rack. Discard if bars develop orange oily spots (DOS) or smell sharp and rancid rather than spiced.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oil left unsaponified in the finished bar. A 5% superfat leaves free conditioning oil.
The moment when the soap batter thickens to a warm-custard consistency. The point to add essential oils before pouring.

content.text: Cinnamon bark and clove bud essential oils are both skin sensitisers: they carry a high eugenol or cinnamaldehyde content that causes reactions at rates that work fine in a diffuser or simmer pot. In a soap bar, the combined usage rate stays at 0.5% of oil weight (2.5 g per 500 g oil batch), which gives a recognisable warm spice note in the finished bar without the irritation risk of higher rates. Ground cinnamon stirred into the batter at
adds warm-brown flecks throughout the bar. Clove bud is known to accelerate trace rapidly; add the essential oil blend at the very last moment and pour immediately. At 5%
with a 67/28/5 olive, coconut, castor base, this is a body soap, not a facial soap.

items.prepNote: 28% of oil weight; melted if solid
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat
0.3% of oil weight; skin sensitiser, do not exceed this rate
0.2% of oil weight; strong trace accelerator; add at the very last moment before pouring

content.text: Put on protective gloves and goggles. Work in a ventilated space. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Add the sodium hydroxide slowly and steadily, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never add water to lye. Cool to 38 to 42°C.
Weigh the olive oil and castor oil into a stainless-steel bowl. Melt the coconut oil and add. Cool the oil blend to 38 to 42°C.
Blend to trace and add the spice blend
Pour the lye solution into the oils in a steady stream. Blend in short 5-second bursts until the batter is at light

. Pre-mix the cinnamon bark and clove bud essential oils in a small cup. Stir them into the batter and give one brief 3-second pulse with the stick blender. Pour immediately into the silicone mould; clove bud accelerates trace and the batter will thicken quickly. Tap the mould once to level the surface and wrap in a towel.

Unmould after 48 hours. Wear gloves throughout. Cut into bars with the wire cutter. Cure on a rack for 4 to 6 weeks. The warm spice note in the finished bar is subtle; it is present in use rather than when smelling the dry bar.

Store cured bars on an open rack in a cool dark place. Bars keep two years. Avoid using this bar on the face or on broken skin. Discard if DOS (dreaded orange spots) appears, or if the bar smells sharp or paint-like rather than spiced.
